title: Child-Sized Surf Board Adirondack Chair
summary: A scaled-down Adirondack chair with surfboard-shaped arms — the same angled stringers that define a full-size Adirondack, cut for a kid's frame.
description:
Like its full-size counterpart elsewhere in this catalog, this chair's whole geometry comes down to the stringers — the two shaped side pieces that carry the seat slope and the seat back angle. Every angle on them is 15° off square in one direction or another, and the source is right to flag this as the step to get right: mark both stringers from the same pattern, cut them together if you can, and confirm they match before building anything else. A mismatched pair means a chair that rocks or sits crooked.

The surfboard detail lives in the arms — shaped and rounded rather than left as plain rectangular boards — and they're one of the last things attached, so there's no rush to get their shape perfect before the rest of the chair is solid. Fastening from the underside where possible keeps the show faces clean, the same trick used on the adult-size version.

tools.note: Shapes the surfboard-style arms and any decorative cutouts.

cutList.note: All angles are 15° off square — mark and cut both together so they match exactly.
Shape and round with a jigsaw for the surfboard look.

steps.title: Cut and match the stringers
steps.body: Getting the stringers right is the most important part of this build. Mark both from the same pattern, using a protractor to draw every angled line — all lines are 15° off square, in one direction or another. Cut both stringers and confirm they match exactly before continuing; a mismatched pair rocks or sits crooked. Mark the front edge of each stringer clearly so you don't lose track of orientation.

steps.title: Attach the front apron
steps.body: Attach the front apron to the stringers with screws and glue.

steps.title: Lay the seat slats
steps.body: Lay the front 1x3 seat slat flush to the back of the cut stringer. Follow with the 1x2 slats, leaving 1/4" gaps between each.

steps.title: Attach the front legs
steps.body: Mark the front legs for seat height on their inside faces. Attach to the stringers and front apron with screws and glue — use 1-1/4" screws into the stringers and 3" screws into the front apron. Fasten from the inside and underside of the chair where possible to keep screws hidden.

steps.title: Attach the seat back
steps.body: Use 2" screws and glue to attach the stringers to the seat back — the chair really starts to take shape at this step.

steps.title: Add the arm supports
steps.body: Leave 3/4" of clearance at the front of the arm supports for the arms to land on later. Attach with 1-1/4" screws to the front legs and 2" screws to the back support.

steps.title: Shape and attach the arms
steps.body: Shape the arms into a surfboard silhouette with the jigsaw — rounded ends, tapered outline. Attach so they tie into both the arm supports and the seat back support, screwing or nailing with glue.

steps.title: Add the decorative supports and finish
steps.body: Glue and nail the decorative arm supports in place to complete the chair's styling. Sand everything smooth and finish with exterior paint or a clear sealer.

images.alt: Child-sized Adirondack chair with surfboard-shaped arms
